topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy flyer for the relocation firm lay crumpled on the coffee table, a stark white intrusion into the muted gray of the apartment. Liam stared at the fine print, his jaw tight enough to crack a tooth. Maya was moving. Across the country. The realization didn't hit him with a crash, but rather a slow, heavy settling, like a stone dropping into deep water. He picked up his phone, his thumb hovering over her contact, then letting it drop back onto the wood surface. A low, almost inaudible sigh escaped him, a sound of profound deflation. He traced the outline of a crack in the tabletop with his fingertip, his gaze fixed on the dust motes dancing in the weak afternoon light. He felt a strange numbness spreading through his chest, making it hard to draw a full breath.

[story 2]

"So, you're actually going?" Chloe asked, her voice bright and utterly oblivious to the atmosphere clinging to Alex. He just nodded, a small, jerky movement. He kept fiddling with the frayed edge of his sweater, the texture rough against his skin. Every time he looked up, his eyes landed on the window, watching the familiar street below as if expecting the world to simply dissolve. The news had landed hours ago, and now it just sat there, a heavy, unmovable weight in his gut. He shifted his weight from one foot to the other, the movement stiff, unwilling to engage with the reality of the impending distance. A tightness coiled in his shoulders, making him want to curl inward, to become invisible.

[story 3]

I reread the email for the fifth time. It was all logistical details, dates, and packing lists—nothing that explained the seismic shift happening inside me. My hands felt cold, clammy, as I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles were white. Every word felt sharp, cutting through the air, emphasizing the emptiness that was rapidly expanding where my usual comfort used to reside. I didn't look up when Sarah finally walked into the room, her cheerful greeting sounding ridiculously loud. I just stared at my reflection in the dark screen of my laptop, noting the flatness of my expression, the way my shoulders seemed permanently hunched forward, as if bracing for a blow that never quite landed.
